content:
You mentioned the Iceberg Theory—that most of a story (or a person) is unseen.

This extends to reality itself:
• We never truly know people—we only see the parts they choose to reveal.
• Our perception of reality is flawed, filtered through limited senses.
• This applies to art as well: the more we hide, the more we reveal.

📌 Examples of Hidden Depths in Whiplash
• Fletcher never outright states why he’s so brutal. Only through small moments (his sadness when discussing a former student’s death) do we see glimpses of humanity.
• Andrew’s internal monologue is never explained—but we see it in his eyes, posture, and small choices.

🔥 Key Writing Rule:
👉 Don’t over-explain. Let themes emerge naturally through character choices and subtext.

3. Thematic Discovery (Tarantino’s Reservoir Dogs)

Tarantino didn’t set out to make Reservoir Dogs a father-son story, but it turned out that way because he trusted his instincts.
context: From document: Analyzing the Common Threads in Whiplash, Real, and Vagabond — chunk 6 (chunk 6/14)
